en: I gotta go back to Jim Webb and that book. What a badass, fascinating book. Oh my god.
ko: 짐 웹과 그 책으로 돌아가야겠어요. 정말 대단하고 매력적인 책이에요.

en: War and Fighting. How the Scots-Irish shaped America.
ko: 전쟁과 싸움. 스코트-아일랜드계가 미국을 어떻게 만들었는지.

en: So, I did not realize to the degree, first of all how badass the Scots-Irish are, and to the degree many of the things that kind of identify as American and part of the American spirit were defined by this relatively small group of people.
ko: 먼저 스코트-아일랜드계가 얼마나 대단한지, 그리고 미국적이라고 생각하는 많은 것들이 이 상대적으로 작은 그룹에 의해 정의됐다는 걸 이 정도인 줄 몰랐어요.

en: As he describes, the model could be summarized as fight, sing, drink, and pray.
ko: 그가 묘사한 모델은 싸우고, 노래하고, 마시고, 기도하는 것으로 요약돼요.

en: So there's the principles of fierce individualism, the principles of a deep distrust of government, the elites, the authorities, bottom-up governance, over 2,000 years of a military tradition.
ko: 치열한 개인주의, 정부와 엘리트에 대한 깊은 불신, 상향식 거버넌스, 2천 년 이상의 군사 전통이에요.

en: They made up 40% of the Revolutionary War army and produced numerous military leaders including Stonewall Jackson, Ulysses S.
ko: 독립전쟁 군대의 40%를 차지했고 스톤월 잭슨, 율리시스 그랜트,

en: Grant, George S. Patton, and a bunch of presidents. Some of the more gangster presidents. Andrew Jackson, Teddy Roosevelt, Woodrow Wilson, Ronald Reagan, and Bill Clinton.
ko: 조지 패튼, 그리고 여러 대통령을 배출했어요. 더 강한 대통령들이요. 앤드루 잭슨, 테디 루즈벨트, 우드로 윌슨, 로널드 레이건, 빌 클린턴.

en: Just the whole cultural legacy of country music. We owe them so much and they really don't get their due, unfortunately.
ko: 컨트리 음악의 문화적 유산 전체. 빚이 많은데 제대로 인정받지 못해요.

en: A lot of it for the reasons that I just described around racialism is because post-mass immigration from Europe, the term white kind of became blanket applied to new Irish, to Italians, to Slovenians.
ko: 인종주의 관련해서 설명한 이유 때문인데, 유럽 대량 이민 후에 백인이라는 용어가 새 아일랜드인, 이탈리아인, 슬로베니아인에게 일괄 적용됐거든요.

en: As you and I both know, if you travel those countries, people are pretty different and it's not that different here in the United States.
ko: 그 나라들을 여행하면 사람들이 꽤 다르고 여기서도 마찬가지예요.

en: Scots-Irish was some of the original settlers here in America and particularly in Appalachia and their contribution to the fighting spirit and their own culture and who we are as individualists and some of the first people to ever settle the frontier and that frontier mindset really does come from them.
ko: 스코트-아일랜드계는 미국, 특히 애팔래치아의 초기 정착민이었고 투쟁 정신과 그들의 문화, 개인주의자로서의 정체성, 개척지를 처음 개척한 사람들로서의 개척 정신이 정말 그들에게서 왔어요.

en: We owe them just as much as we do the Puritans, but they don't ever really get their due.
ko: 퓨리턴만큼이나 빚지고 있지만 제대로 인정받지 못해요.

en: The reason I recommend that book is if you read that book and you understand then how exactly could this group of white working class voters forego from 2012 voting for a man named Barack Hussein Obama to Donald J.
ko: 이 책을 추천하는 이유는 이 책을 읽으면 어떻게 이 백인 노동 계급 유권자들이 2012년 버락 후세인 오바마에게 투표하다가 도널드 트럼프에게 투표할 수 있었는지 완벽히 이해돼요.

en: Trump, it makes perfect sense if you combine it with a lot of the stuff I'm talking about here about affirmative action and about distrust of the elites, about feeling as if institutions are not seeing through to you and specifically also not valuing your contribution to American history and in some cases actively looking down.
ko: 소수우대정책, 엘리트 불신, 제도가 당신을 보지 못한다는 느낌, 미국 역사에 대한 기여를 인정하지 않고 무시하는 것과 결합하면 완벽히 말이 돼요.

en: I'm glad you pointed out not only their role in the Revolutionary War, but in the Civil War as well and just how much of a contribution culturally really that we owe them for setting the groundwork that so many of us who came later could build upon and adopt some of their own ideas and their culture as our own.
ko: 독립전쟁뿐 아니라 남북전쟁에서의 역할과 문화적 기여를 지적해주셔서 감사해요. 나중에 온 우리가 그들의 아이디어와 문화를 우리 것으로 채택할 수 있는 토대를 놓은 거예요.

en: It's one of the things that makes America great. Marc Twain. Yeah.
ko: 미국을 위대하게 만드는 것 중 하나예요. 마크 트웨인.

en: I mean, so much of the culture, so much of the American spirit, the whole idea, the whole shape and form and type of populism that represents our democracy.
ko: 문화의 많은 부분, 미국 정신의 많은 부분, 우리 민주주의를 대표하는 포퓰리즘의 형태가.

en: Would you trace that fierce individualism that we think of back to them?
ko: 그 치열한 개인주의를 그들에게서 추적할 수 있나요?

en: Definitely. It's a huge part of them about who they were, about the screw you attitude. I mean, that book actually kind of had a renaissance back in 2016 when Hillbilly Elegy came out.
ko: 확실히요. 그들이 누군지의 큰 부분이에요. 까라 하는 태도. 그 책이 2016년 힐빌리 엘레지가 나왔을 때 르네상스를 겪었어요.

en: I'm sure you remember this, which it's kind of weird to think that it's now the vice president-elect of the United States.
ko: 지금 부통령 당선인이라는 게 좀 이상하죠.

en: It's kind of wild, honestly, to think about. But J.D.
ko: 솔직히 좀 초현실적이에요. 2017년에 바에서 JD 밴스를 만났는데.

en: Vance's book, Hillbilly Elegy, I think was really important for a lot of American elites who were like, how do these people support Trump?
ko: 부통령 당선인이 될 줄은 몰랐어요. 좀 미쳤어요. 원래 보낸 이메일을 친구가 찾아냈는데, 만나실래요? 했더니 좋다고.

en: Where does this shit come from? If you really think back to that time, it was shocking to the elite character that any person in the world could ever vote for Donald Trump and not just vote.
ko: TV에서 봤을 때 처음 실감했어요. 부통령 후보로 지명됐을 때. 색종이가 떨어지고 아내와 손 흔들고 있는데, 와, 역사책에 남는 거구나.

en: He won the election. How does that happen? And that's Hillbilly Elegy guided people in an understanding of what that's like on a lived day-to-day basis.
ko: 특히 지금 부통령 당선인으로서. 하지만 그의 진화 자체가 매력적인 이야기예요.

en: And J.D., to his credit, talks about the Scots-Irish heritage, about Appalachia and the legacy of what that culture looks like today and how a lot of these people voted for Donald Trump.
ko: 지금 제가 많이 하는 건 2016년 트럼프가 왜 이겼는지에 대한 관점이에요.

en: But we got to give credit to Jim Webb, who wrote the history of these people and taught me and you about their original fight against the oppressors in Scotland and Ireland and their militant spirit and how they were able to bring that over here.
ko: 하지만 이번 선거에서 트럼프가 어떻게 이겼는지, 바이든 행정부의 실패, 각성주의에 대한 반작용도 얘기해야 해요.

en: And they got their due in Andrew Jackson and some of our other populist presidents who set us up on the road to Donald Trump to where we are today.
ko: JD 밴스의 진화를 보면, 힐빌리 엘레지를 쓴 사람이에요.

en: Dude, it got me pumped, excited to be an American. Me too. I love that book.
ko: 읽어보면 JD는 당시 더 전통적인 보수주의자였어요.

en: It's crazy that J.D., the same guy, because that's Hillbilly Elegy is what I kind of thought of him as.
ko: 대출에 대한 유명한 구절이 있는데. 지금 입장은 모르겠지만 아마 바뀌었을 거예요.

en: I'll tell you, for me, it's actually pretty surreal. I met J.D. Vance in 2017 in a bar.
ko: 요점은 백인 노동 계급에 대한 깊은 공감을 가진 전통적 공화당원에서 트럼프의 제자가 되고 자신이 미국을 개선할 도구라고 믿게 된 이데올로기적 진화예요.

en: I didn't ever think he would be the vice president-elect of the United States. It's kind of wild. One of my friends went back and dug up the email that we originally sent him, just like, hey, do you want to meet up?
ko: 그 이야기는 현대 좌파가 정치적 프로젝트이자 이데올로기로서 부상한 것이에요.

en: And he was like, sure. I was watching on television. The first time that it really hit me, I was like, whoa.
ko: 공화당이 트럼프를 합법적 인물로 결집해서 문화적 좌파주의와 엘리트주의에 대한 유일한 보루가 되어 2024년 미국인 다수가 투표한 거예요.

en: It was like naming a history book. It's whenever he became the vice presidential nominee. I was watching him on TV and the confetti was falling and he was waving with his wife and I was like, wow, that's it.

You're in the history books now, forever, especially now as the literal vice president-elect of the U.S. But his own evolution is actually a fascinating story for us, too, because I think a lot of the time I've spent right now is kind of... A lot of what I'm giving right now are 2016 kind of takes about why Trump won that time.
But we should spend a lot of time on how Donald Trump won this election and how what happened, some of the failures of the Biden administration, some of the payback for the great awokening.
But also, if you look at the evolution of J.D. Vance, this is a person who wrote Hillbilly Elegy.
And not a lot of people pay attention to this, but if you read Hillbilly Elegy, J.D. was much more of a traditional conservative at that time.
He was citing... I think the famous passage is about payday loans and why they're good or something like that.
I don't know his position today, but I would assume that he's probably changed that.

But the point is, is that his ideological evolution from watching somebody who really was more of a traditional Republican with a deep empathy for the white working class, then eventually become a champion and a disciple of Donald Trump and to believe that he himself was the vehicle for accomplishing and bettering the United States, but specifically for working class Americans, really, of all stripes.
And that story is really one of the rise of the modern left as it exists as a political project, as an ideology.

It's also one of the Republican Party, which coalesced now with Donald Trump as a legitimate figure and as the single bulwark against cultural leftism and elitism that eventually was normalized to the point that majority of Americans decided to vote for him in 2024.
